county_metadata.parent_county: Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands (US administration, 1947-1978)
county_metadata.record_losses: The Northern Islands municipality encompasses the uninhabited volcanic islands north of Saipan (Agrihan, Alamagan, Anatahan, Asuncion, Guguan, Maug, Pagan, Sarigan, and Farallon de Pajaros). These islands had small populations during Spanish, German, and Japanese periods but were evacuated during WWII and never permanently resettled. Civil records are virtually nonexistent; any historical records are subsumed under Saipan administration.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Northern Islands Municipality was established in 1978 with the creation of the CNMI Commonwealth government. It is an administrative unit encompassing all islands north of Saipan: Agrihan, Alamagan, Anatahan, Asuncion, Farallon de Pajaros (Uracas), Guguan, Maug Islands, Pagan, and Sarigan. These volcanic islands had small, transient populations during the Spanish, German, and Japanese colonial periods -- primarily Carolinian copra workers and Japanese settlers on Pagan and Agrihan. All residents were evacuated during WWII. Post-war resettlement attempts on Pagan were interrupted by volcanic eruptions (1981 Pagan eruption forced final evacuation). The municipality has no permanent residents and no local government infrastructure. For CNMI legislative purposes, Northern Islands has a representative in the CNMI House of Representatives.
county_metadata.research_tips: The Northern Islands municipality is an administrative unit encompassing the uninhabited volcanic islands north of Saipan, including Pagan, Agrihan, Alamagan, and Anatahan. These islands had very small populations during Spanish, German, and Japanese colonial periods -- mostly Carolinian copra workers and Japanese settlers on Pagan and Agrihan. All residents were evacuated during WWII and permanent settlement has not resumed due to volcanic activity and lack of infrastructure. For genealogical research, any records relating to Northern Islands residents would be found under Saipan's administration, in Catholic Church records (since missionaries occasionally visited), or in Japanese colonial administrative records at the National Diet Library in Tokyo. The Trust Territory Archives at the University of Hawaii at Manoa may contain some post-war administrative references. The CNMI Historic Preservation Office in Saipan maintains whatever historical documentation exists. This is the most limited municipality for genealogical research -- researchers should focus on Saipan records and Japanese colonial archives for any Northern Islands family connections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: No online land records exist for the Northern Islands municipality; the islands are uninhabited and land matters are administered through CNMI government offices on Saipan

source_profiles.source_name: Catholic Mission Records for the Northern Islands -- Capuchin/Jesuit Archives
source_profiles.repository: Archdiocese of Agana (Guam) / Capuchin Archives / Diocese of Chalan Kanoa
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Sporadic Catholic missionary visits to the Northern Islands during the Spanish period (1668-1899); any sacramental records (baptisms, marriages, burials) would be recorded under the broader Marianas mission. Coverage is extremely sparse -- most Northern Islands residents were Carolinian copra workers who may appear in Saipan parish records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Virtually no records specific to the Northern Islands survive; any extant records are subsumed under Saipan or Guam parish files

source_profiles.source_name: Trust Territory Government Records -- Northern Islands -- University of Hawaii
source_profiles.repository: Trust Territory Archives, University of Hawaii at Manoa
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands administrative records for the Mariana Islands district, which includes references to Northern Islands; includes land matters, population counts, and administrative correspondence; Northern Islands content is minimal due to very small and transient population
source_profiles.survival_notes: Trust Territory Archives at University of Hawaii hold the most comprehensive post-war administrative records; Northern Islands-specific material is embedded within broader Mariana Islands district files

source_profiles.source_name: Northern Islands Census and Population Records -- CNMI / Trust Territory
source_profiles.repository: CNMI Central Statistics Division / Trust Territory Archives, University of Hawaii at Manoa
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Japanese colonial census data (1920, 1930, 1935) may include Northern Islands residents under Saipan district; US Census Bureau enumerated CNMI starting 1980 but Northern Islands had zero permanent population; Trust Territory population surveys from 1958-1973 recorded small numbers on Pagan and Agrihan
source_profiles.survival_notes: Census records for the Northern Islands are fragmentary; Japanese-era census records are at the National Diet Library in Tokyo; post-war population data is in Trust Territory reports

source_profiles.source_name: WWII Military Records -- Northern Islands -- NARA
source_profiles.repository: National Archives and Records Administration (NARA), College Park, MD
source_profiles.coverage_notes: US military operational records for the Northern Mariana Islands campaign; includes reconnaissance reports, post-war surveys of the Northern Islands, and evacuation records; Japanese military garrison records for Pagan were destroyed or captured
source_profiles.survival_notes: US military records at NARA are well-preserved; Japanese military records for the Northern Islands are largely destroyed

county_metadata.parent_county: Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands (US administration, 1947-1978)
county_metadata.record_losses: Rota was bypassed by US forces during WWII and did not experience the devastating combat that struck Saipan and Tinian. Japanese administration maintained some civil records (1914-1944) but most were destroyed or removed at the end of the war. Spanish-era records are fragmentary and largely limited to Catholic Church sacramental registers. Pre-WWII civil records are sparse but Rota's physical infrastructure and some local records survived the war largely intact.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Rota Municipality (also known as Luta in Chamorro) was established in 1978 with the CNMI Commonwealth government. Rota is the southernmost of the CNMI's inhabited islands, located about 40 miles north of Guam. The island has been continuously inhabited by Chamorro people for millennia. Spanish missionaries arrived in 1668 as part of the Marianas mission. During the Spanish Reduccion (forced relocation policy, late 1600s-early 1700s), many Rota Chamorros were relocated to Guam, but some remained or returned. Rota passed from Spain to Germany (1899), then Japan (1914), then the US Trust Territory (1947). Uniquely, Rota was bypassed by US forces during WWII and did not experience the combat destruction that devastated Saipan and Tinian. The Japanese garrison surrendered peacefully in September 1945. Municipal seat is Songsong village. Population is predominantly Chamorro with Carolinian and Filipino communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: Rota is unique among the main CNMI islands because it was bypassed during WWII, meaning its physical landscape and some local records survived relatively intact. The primary genealogical sources are: (1) Catholic Church records at San Francisco de Borja Church in Songsong, which has baptism, marriage, and burial registers extending back to the Spanish colonial period -- these are the single most important source for Rota families; (2) CNMI Vital Records Office on Saipan for post-1952 births, deaths, and marriages; (3) Trust Territory Archives at the University of Hawaii at Manoa for post-1947 administrative records including vital statistics and land records; (4) Japanese colonial records at the National Diet Library in Tokyo and the Japan Center for Asian Historical Records (JACAR) -- the Japanese Nan'yo-cho (South Seas Bureau) administered Rota from 1914-1944 and kept family registers (koseki) for Japanese nationals and some records for Chamorro and Carolinian families; (5) German colonial records (1899-1914) are sparse but may be found at the German Federal Archives (Bundesarchiv) in Berlin-Lichterfelde. Rota's Chamorro population maintained continuous residence through all colonial periods, making oral history and family Bible records particularly valuable. The CNMI Historic Preservation Office and the Northern Mariana Islands Museum of History and Culture on Saipan hold some Rota materials.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Rota land records are maintained by the CNMI Department of Public Lands and the Commonwealth Recorder on Saipan; Trust Territory land records from 1947 at the Trust Territory Archives, University of Hawaii; no online access to historical land records

source_profiles.source_name: San Francisco de Borja Church Sacramental Records -- Rota
source_profiles.repository: San Francisco de Borja Parish, Songsong, Rota / Diocese of Chalan Kanoa / Archdiocese of Agana (Guam)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic sacramental registers (baptisms, marriages, burials) from the Spanish mission period through the present. Rota's Catholic parish is the most important genealogical source for Rota families, as it provides continuous documentation through Spanish, German, Japanese, and American periods. The parish was served by Jesuits (1668-1769), Augustinian Recollects (1769-1899), and Capuchins (from 1907). Earlier records may be copies or extracts from the Guam mission records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Rota was bypassed in WWII, so church records survived better than on Saipan or Tinian; however, earlier Spanish-era records are fragmentary and some may have been transferred to Guam or lost during colonial transitions

source_profiles.source_name: Rota Census and Population Records -- Japanese/Trust Territory/US Census
source_profiles.repository: National Diet Library, Tokyo / Trust Territory Archives, University of Hawaii / US Census Bureau
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish colonial population counts from the early 1700s (padron/census records in missionary reports); Japanese colonial census (1920, 1925, 1930, 1935) enumerated Rota's population including Japanese settlers and Chamorro residents; Trust Territory population surveys (1958, 1967, 1973); US Census Bureau enumerated CNMI from 1980 onward (1980, 1990, 2000, 2010, 2020)
source_profiles.survival_notes: Spanish-era population counts are scattered in missionary correspondence and government reports; Japanese census records are at the National Diet Library; Trust Territory data at University of Hawaii

source_profiles.source_name: Rota Land Records -- CNMI Commonwealth Recorder / Trust Territory Archives
source_profiles.repository: CNMI Commonwealth Recorder, Saipan / Trust Territory Archives, University of Hawaii at Manoa
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Trust Territory land records from 1947 documenting land ownership, homestead grants, and property transfers on Rota; the Trust Territory government conducted land surveys and adjudicated traditional land claims; post-1978 records at the CNMI Commonwealth Recorder; pre-WWII land tenure was largely customary (Chamorro matrilineal inheritance traditions)
source_profiles.survival_notes: Trust Territory land records are at the University of Hawaii and CNMI government offices; pre-Trust Territory land records are virtually nonexistent as formal documentation; traditional Chamorro land tenure was oral

source_profiles.source_name: Rota Vital Records -- CNMI Vital Records Office
source_profiles.repository: CNMI Vital Records Office, Commonwealth Health Center, Saipan / Rota Health Center
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Birth, death, and marriage certificates from approximately 1952 when the Trust Territory government established civil registration in the Mariana Islands district; earlier vital events must be reconstructed from Catholic Church sacramental records; the CNMI Vital Records Office on Saipan is the central repository
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil vital records are substantially complete from the early 1950s; registration compliance improved over time; pre-1952 vital events are documented only in church records

source_profiles.source_name: Trust Territory Court Records -- Rota -- University of Hawaii
source_profiles.repository: Trust Territory Archives, University of Hawaii at Manoa / CNMI Superior Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Trust Territory district court records for the Mariana Islands district, including Rota cases -- adoption records, estate matters, land disputes, and civil cases; post-1978 records at CNMI Superior Court; court records are particularly valuable for documenting family relationships and land inheritance
source_profiles.survival_notes: Trust Territory court records are at the University of Hawaii; post-1978 CNMI Superior Court records are maintained on Saipan

source_profiles.source_name: WWII and Japanese Colonial Military Records -- Rota -- NARA / JACAR
source_profiles.repository: NARA, College Park, MD / Japan Center for Asian Historical Records (JACAR)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Japanese military garrison records for Rota during the mandate period and WWII; US military intelligence reports and civil affairs records for Rota after the Japanese surrender (September 1945); Rota was not invaded but the garrison surrendered peacefully; NARA holds US military administration records; JACAR has digitized Japanese colonial and military records
source_profiles.survival_notes: US military civil affairs records at NARA are well-preserved; Japanese military records for Rota are partially surviving at JACAR

county_metadata.parent_county: Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands (US administration, 1947-1978)
county_metadata.record_losses: The Battle of Saipan (June 15 - July 9, 1944) caused catastrophic destruction across the island. Japanese military and civil administration records were largely destroyed during the battle or deliberately burned by retreating Japanese forces. The pre-war Japanese civilian population of approximately 25,000 (vs. about 4,000 Chamorros) was repatriated. Spanish colonial records had already been transferred or lost during the German and Japanese transitions. Virtually no civil records survive from before 1944. The CNMI Historic Preservation Office and surviving Catholic Church records are the primary sources for pre-war history.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Saipan Municipality was established in 1978 with the CNMI Commonwealth government. Saipan is the largest and most populous of the Northern Mariana Islands and serves as the CNMI capital (Capitol Hill). Chamorro people inhabited Saipan for thousands of years before Spanish colonization began with the Jesuit mission of Padre Diego Luis de San Vitores in 1668. The Spanish-Chamorro Wars (1672-1698) and the Reduccion policy devastated the indigenous population, reducing it from an estimated 40,000-50,000 across the Marianas to fewer than 4,000. Germany administered Saipan from 1899-1914, introducing copra plantations. Japan received the island as a League of Nations mandate after WWI and transformed it through intensive sugar cultivation -- by the 1930s, the Japanese civilian population (~25,000) far outnumbered the Chamorro population (~4,000). The Battle of Saipan (June 15 - July 9, 1944) was among the bloodiest of the Pacific War, with catastrophic casualties among Japanese military, Japanese civilians (including mass suicides at Banzai Cliff and Suicide Cliff), and Chamorro civilians. Post-war, the US administered Saipan under the Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands. The CNMI Covenant was signed in 1975 and became effective in 1978.
county_metadata.research_tips: Saipan is the CNMI capital and most populous island, but WWII devastation means pre-1944 records are extremely scarce. Key research strategies: (1) Catholic Church records are the most important early source -- the Kristo Rai Cathedral (formerly Our Lady of Mount Carmel) parish in Chalan Kanoa has sacramental registers, though many were destroyed in the 1944 battle; surviving records and copies may be at the Archdiocese of Agana (Guam) or the Capuchin archives, as Capuchin friars served the Marianas from 1907; (2) CNMI Vital Records Office for post-1952 registrations; (3) Trust Territory Archives at the University of Hawaii at Manoa -- the most important single repository for post-WWII CNMI records, holding Trust Territory government files (1947-1978) including vital statistics, court records, land records, and administrative correspondence; (4) NARA (National Archives) in College Park, MD and San Bruno, CA holds US Navy and Department of Interior Trust Territory records; (5) Japanese colonial records at the National Diet Library, JACAR, and Nan'yo-cho records -- Japanese family registers (koseki) documented the large Japanese settler community; (6) Spanish colonial records may survive in the Archivo General de Indias (Seville) and Archivo General Militar (Madrid) -- missionary reports, baptism records, and population counts from the 1668-1899 period; (7) CNMI Historic Preservation Office maintains archaeological and historical records; (8) Northern Mariana Islands Museum of History and Culture in Garapan holds photographs, oral histories, and artifacts. Chamorro and Carolinian oral traditions and clan genealogies (i familian) are critical supplements to written records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Saipan land records are maintained by the CNMI Commonwealth Recorder and Department of Public Lands; Trust Territory land records from 1947 at the Trust Territory Archives, University of Hawaii; Japanese-era land records were largely destroyed in WWII; no online portal for historical land records

source_profiles.source_name: Kristo Rai Cathedral and Catholic Parish Records -- Saipan
source_profiles.repository: Diocese of Chalan Kanoa / Kristo Rai Cathedral, Saipan / Archdiocese of Agana (Guam) / Capuchin Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic sacramental registers (baptisms, marriages, burials) from the earliest Jesuit mission (Padre San Vitores, 1668). The Capuchin friars who served the Marianas from 1907 maintained records through the Japanese period and WWII. Saipan's Catholic parish records are the single most important source for pre-WWII Chamorro families. Some records may be at the Archdiocese of Agana (Guam), which historically administered the Marianas mission, or in Capuchin archives in Europe (Spain/Italy)
source_profiles.survival_notes: Many pre-WWII church records were destroyed in the Battle of Saipan (1944); surviving records are fragmentary for the Spanish and German periods; Capuchin records from 1907-1944 may have partial survival through copies sent to the order's archives; post-war records are more complete

source_profiles.source_name: Saipan Vital Records -- CNMI Vital Records Office
source_profiles.repository: CNMI Vital Records Office, Commonwealth Health Center, Saipan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Birth, death, and marriage certificates from approximately 1945 when US military administration began civil registration on Saipan, continued by the Trust Territory government from 1947; the CNMI Vital Records Office is the central repository for all CNMI vital records; earlier vital events are documented only in Catholic Church sacramental records or Japanese family registers (koseki)
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil vital records from 1945 onward are substantially complete; US military civil affairs teams began registration immediately after the battle; formal Trust Territory registration improved compliance from the early 1950s

source_profiles.source_name: Saipan Land Records -- CNMI Commonwealth Recorder / Trust Territory Archives
source_profiles.repository: CNMI Commonwealth Recorder, Saipan / Trust Territory Archives, University of Hawaii at Manoa / CNMI Department of Public Lands
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Trust Territory land records from 1947 including homestead grants, land surveys, and adjudication of traditional land claims on Saipan; the Trust Territory government attempted to formalize Chamorro customary land tenure; post-1978 records at CNMI Commonwealth Recorder; Japanese-era land records (sugar company land, government land) were largely destroyed in WWII
source_profiles.survival_notes: Trust Territory land records at the University of Hawaii and CNMI offices are the earliest systematic land documentation; pre-WWII land records were destroyed; Chamorro traditional land tenure relied on oral transmission through matrilineal clan systems

source_profiles.source_name: Saipan Census and Population Records -- Spanish/Japanese/Trust Territory/US
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Indias, Seville / National Diet Library, Tokyo / Trust Territory Archives, University of Hawaii / US Census Bureau
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish colonial population counts (padrones) from the early 1700s in missionary and government reports; German-era population records (1899-1914); Japanese colonial census data (1920, 1925, 1930, 1935) -- these are especially important as they document the large Japanese settler community and the Chamorro/Carolinian population; Trust Territory surveys (1958, 1967, 1973); US Census from 1980
source_profiles.survival_notes: Spanish padrones are scattered in archives in Seville and Madrid; Japanese census records at the National Diet Library are the most systematic pre-war population data; Trust Territory surveys at University of Hawaii

source_profiles.source_name: Trust Territory and CNMI Court Records -- Saipan
source_profiles.repository: Trust Territory Archives, University of Hawaii at Manoa / CNMI Superior Court, Saipan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Trust Territory High Court and district court records for the Mariana Islands, including adoptions (both customary and legal), probate matters, land disputes, and civil cases; post-1978 records at CNMI Superior Court and Supreme Court; court records are essential for documenting family relationships, inheritance patterns, and customary adoption (a widespread Chamorro and Carolinian practice)
source_profiles.survival_notes: Trust Territory court records are well-preserved at the University of Hawaii; CNMI court records from 1978 onward are maintained by the CNMI judiciary

source_profiles.source_name: Battle of Saipan and WWII Military Records -- NARA
source_profiles.repository: National Archives and Records Administration (NARA), College Park, MD / NARA San Bruno, CA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: US military records of the Battle of Saipan (June-July 1944), post-battle civil affairs records, internment camp records for Chamorro civilians at Camp Susupe, repatriation records for Japanese civilians, US Navy military government records (1944-1947), and early Trust Territory administrative records; these records document the immediate post-war period and are critical for understanding the displacement and reconstitution of Chamorro families
source_profiles.survival_notes: US military and civil affairs records at NARA are comprehensive; Camp Susupe internment records document Chamorro families during the post-battle period; Japanese military records were largely destroyed

county_metadata.parent_county: Trust Territory of the Pacific Islands (US administration, 1947-1978)
county_metadata.record_losses: The Battle of Tinian (July 24 - August 1, 1944) resulted in severe destruction. Japanese sugar industry records and civil administration files were destroyed during the battle. After the war, the US military occupied most of Tinian for North Field (the airbase from which atomic bomb missions launched). The civilian population was confined to a small area. Pre-war records were almost entirely lost. Spanish-era records are limited to Catholic Church materials. Japanese-era civil records were destroyed or removed.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Tinian Municipality was established in 1978 with the CNMI Commonwealth government. Tinian is located about 5 miles southwest of Saipan. Ancient Chamorro civilization on Tinian is evidenced by the House of Taga, a massive latte stone structure dating to approximately 1000 AD. Spanish missionaries arrived in the late 1600s, and Tinian's population was decimated by the Reduccion policy and disease -- by the early 1700s, the island was nearly depopulated. Spain used Tinian as a cattle ranch. During the German period (1899-1914), Carolinians from the central Caroline Islands were resettled on Tinian. Under Japanese mandate (1914-1944), the Nanyo Kohatsu Kaisha (South Seas Development Company) established extensive sugar plantations, bringing Japanese and Okinawan workers who became the majority population. The Battle of Tinian (July 24 - August 1, 1944) resulted in the destruction of most infrastructure. After the battle, the US military built North Field, the largest airbase in the Pacific -- from which the Enola Gay (August 6, 1945) and Bockscar (August 9, 1945) launched the atomic bomb missions against Hiroshima and Nagasaki. Most of Tinian remained under US military control for decades after the war. Municipal seat is San Jose.
county_metadata.research_tips: Tinian's genealogical records were heavily impacted by WWII destruction and subsequent US military occupation of most of the island. Research strategies: (1) Catholic Church records at San Jose Church in Tinian -- the parish has maintained sacramental registers, though pre-WWII records were largely destroyed; the Diocese of Chalan Kanoa (established 1984, formerly under Agana) may hold copies or reconstructed records; (2) CNMI Vital Records Office on Saipan for post-1952 registrations; (3) Trust Territory Archives at the University of Hawaii at Manoa for post-1947 government records; (4) NARA for Trust Territory and US military administration records -- Tinian was under direct US Navy control for years after WWII due to North Field's strategic importance (the base from which the Enola Gay and Bockscar launched the atomic bomb missions); (5) Japanese colonial records at the National Diet Library and JACAR -- during the Japanese period, Tinian had a large Japanese and Okinawan sugar plantation workforce (the Nanyo Kohatsu Kaisha sugar company ran extensive operations), and the Nan'yo-cho maintained family registers for these communities; (6) Spanish-era records are minimal but the Archivo General de Indias may hold missionary reports; (7) The CNMI Historic Preservation Office has documented Tinian's historic sites including the House of Taga (ancient Chamorro latte stone site) and Japanese-era ruins. Pre-WWII Tinian family research heavily depends on Chamorro oral traditions, surviving Catholic records, and Japanese colonial archives.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tinian land records are maintained by the CNMI Commonwealth Recorder on Saipan; Trust Territory land records from 1947 at the Trust Territory Archives, University of Hawaii; pre-WWII land records were destroyed; US military land use dominated post-war Tinian; no online access

source_profiles.source_name: San Jose Church Sacramental Records -- Tinian
source_profiles.repository: San Jose Parish, Tinian / Diocese of Chalan Kanoa / Archdiocese of Agana (Guam)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic sacramental registers (baptisms, marriages, burials) from the Spanish mission period. Tinian was nearly depopulated during the Spanish Reduccion and was used primarily as a cattle ranch; sacramental records from the Spanish period are extremely sparse. Carolinian resettlement in the early 1800s and Japanese-era records (Capuchin mission from 1907) provide some documentation. The Diocese of Chalan Kanoa or Archdiocese of Agana may hold surviving records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Pre-WWII church records were largely destroyed in the Battle of Tinian (1944); surviving fragments may be in Capuchin order archives or Guam diocesan archives; post-war records are more complete

source_profiles.source_name: Tinian Vital Records -- CNMI Vital Records Office
source_profiles.repository: CNMI Vital Records Office, Commonwealth Health Center, Saipan / Tinian Health Center
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Birth, death, and marriage certificates from approximately 1945 when US military administration established civil registration; continued by the Trust Territory government; Tinian's post-war civilian population was confined to San Jose village while the US military occupied most of the island for North Field operations; CNMI Vital Records Office on Saipan is the central repository
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil vital records from 1945 onward are complete; registration on Tinian was administered through the Saipan district office during the Trust Territory period

source_profiles.source_name: Tinian Land Records -- CNMI Commonwealth Recorder / Trust Territory Archives
source_profiles.repository: CNMI Commonwealth Recorder, Saipan / Trust Territory Archives, University of Hawaii at Manoa / CNMI Department of Public Lands
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Trust Territory land records for Tinian from 1947; land adjudication on Tinian was complicated by US military control of approximately two-thirds of the island for North Field; Trust Territory and later CNMI government negotiated land use agreements; Chamorro traditional land claims were adjudicated through the Trust Territory land commission; Japanese-era sugar company land records were destroyed
source_profiles.survival_notes: Trust Territory land records at the University of Hawaii are the primary source; post-1978 records at CNMI Commonwealth Recorder; pre-WWII land documentation was destroyed

source_profiles.source_name: Tinian Census and Population Records -- Japanese/Trust Territory/US
source_profiles.repository: National Diet Library, Tokyo / Trust Territory Archives, University of Hawaii / US Census Bureau
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Japanese colonial census (1920, 1925, 1930, 1935) documenting the large Japanese/Okinawan sugar plantation workforce and the small Chamorro/Carolinian community; Spanish-era population counts are minimal as Tinian was largely uninhabited; Trust Territory surveys (1958, 1967, 1973); US Census from 1980
source_profiles.survival_notes: Japanese census records at the National Diet Library are the most important pre-war population source for Tinian; Trust Territory data at University of Hawaii; Spanish-era records are scattered

source_profiles.source_name: Battle of Tinian and North Field Military Records -- NARA
source_profiles.repository: National Archives and Records Administration (NARA), College Park, MD / NARA San Bruno, CA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: US military records of the Battle of Tinian (July-August 1944), construction and operation of North Field (the largest Pacific airbase), civil affairs records for Tinian's displaced civilian population, Japanese civilian repatriation records, and post-war military government records; North Field was the launch base for the Enola Gay and Bockscar atomic bomb missions; these records document the displacement of Tinian's Chamorro and Carolinian families
source_profiles.survival_notes: US military records at NARA are comprehensive for the battle and post-war period; Japanese military and Nanyo Kohatsu Kaisha (sugar company) records were largely destroyed
